common_name: Blue Hawaii Elephant Ear (Steel-Blue Metallic Colocasia with Dark Stems)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring after the last frost in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Blue Hawaii's metallic blue-green leaf color intensifies with more light — partial sun (3–4 hours direct) combined with consistently moist soil produces the best color development.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Blue Hawaii is one of the most striking ornamental Colocasia cultivars for Gulf South gardens — producing large, heart-shaped leaves in a remarkable steel-blue to turquoise-blue metallic color with contrasting purple-black stems and dark veining that creates an unusual, jewel-like combination found in very few other garden plants. The metallic blue-green leaf color — a quality that changes slightly with light intensity, becoming more intensely blue in partial sun and more green in deep shade — develops from the waxy, blue-tinted cuticle on the leaf surface and is unlike the green, chartreuse, or black coloration of other commonly grown elephant ear cultivars. Blue Hawaii is more compact than 'Black Magic' or the standard green taro, typically reaching 3–5 feet in garden conditions — a more manageable scale for mixed border use while still providing bold tropical impact. The combination of the metallic blue leaf color and dark stems creates an extraordinary tropical accent that pairs beautifully with golden-leaved companions (gold-leaved hostas, Golden Japanese forest grass) and with vivid orange or red flowering plants where the blue foliage provides striking complementary contrast. In zone 9, Blue Hawaii is perennial, dying back in cold winters and returning reliably from persistent tuberous roots in spring. Consistent moisture — supplemental irrigation during dry periods — is important for maintaining leaf quality and the best metallic color.

common_name: Burning Heart Caladium (Vivid Scarlet-Red Center Fancy-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Burning Heart's vivid scarlet coloration is most intense in bright partial shade — deep shade may cause the red to soften toward pink-orange.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Burning Heart is one of the most vivid and boldly colored red caladiums available — a large fancy-leaf type producing heart-shaped leaves with a dramatic, burning-scarlet to crimson-red center that bleeds and radiates outward from the main veins, creating a fiery effect set against wide, fresh-green margins that frame and intensify the red center by contrast. The intensity of the red in Burning Heart is exceptional among caladiums — a true scarlet rather than the pink or rose tones of many nominally 'red' caladium varieties — creating one of the most vivid color contrasts in the shade garden plant palette. As a large fancy-leaf type, Burning Heart produces the classic large, glossy, heart-shaped leaves reaching 12–18 inches on vigorous plants in optimal conditions. In zone 9, Burning Heart performs reliably through the warm season (May–October), producing the best leaf size and most intense color in the June–August peak heat period when warm soil and air temperatures stimulate maximum growth. The vivid red coloration is outstanding in mass plantings against green groundcovers, as a focal point in mixed shade containers, or combined with the bold white leaves of 'Aaron' or 'Candidum' for maximum red-and-white contrast. Maintain consistent moisture and mulch to stabilize soil temperature and prevent drought stress that causes leaf burn.

common_name: Aaron Caladium (Large White Sun-Tolerant Strap-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Aaron is one of the most sun-tolerant caladium varieties — it tolerates 3–5 hours of direct morning sun without bleaching or burning, expanding its usefulness to lightly shaded positions.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Aaron is one of the oldest, most commercially important, and most reliably grown white caladium varieties — a large strap-leaf type producing elongated, lance-to-heart-shaped leaves with near-white to cream-white leaf surfaces and contrasting green margins, creating a clean, elegant, white-and-green color combination that is among the most versatile in shade garden design. Unlike many white caladiums that require deep shade, Aaron's strap-leaf form and leaf cuticle characteristics give it exceptional sun tolerance — Aaron can be planted in positions receiving 3–5 hours of direct morning sun without the bleaching, edge-burn, or leaf collapse that afflicts most large-leaved white fancy caladiums. This higher sun tolerance makes Aaron useful in transition zones between full shade and partial sun where white color is desired but deep-shade caladiums would stretch and underperform. The white coloration brightens dark shade positions dramatically and creates an elegant effect in formal garden beds, containers, and mass plantings along shaded foundations. Aaron remains one of the most widely produced commercial caladium varieties because of its reliable performance, good tuber production, vigorous growth, and consistent white color. In zone 9, Aaron produces its best growth and color during the warm season from May through October. Combine with 'Burning Heart' for bold red-and-white contrast in shade beds.

common_name: Carolyn Whorton Caladium (Classic Rose-Pink Large Fancy-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Carolyn Whorton is a vigorous, large-tuber-producing variety that improves with successive seasons as tubers increase in size.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Carolyn Whorton is one of the most classic and beloved large fancy-leaf caladium varieties — a vigorous, large-leaved type producing heart-shaped leaves with a rose-pink to warm-pink center that radiates from the primary veins outward across the leaf, set against fresh-green margins with occasional splashes and spots of pink extending into the green zone. The warm, medium-pink tone of Carolyn Whorton differs from the cool pink-lavender of 'Pink Symphony' and the very pale shell-pink of some pastel types — it has a clear, warm, rose-pink quality that reads well at a distance in shade border plantings and has a friendly, cottage-garden quality distinct from the bold drama of red varieties. Carolyn Whorton is a vigorous, proven performer that produces large numbers of daughter tubers over successive seasons, making it one of the best caladiums for naturalizing a permanent in-ground planting in zone 9 where tubers overwinter reliably. Combine Carolyn Whorton with white caladiums ('Aaron', 'Candidum') for a soft pink-and-white romantic effect, or with green-and-white companions (white impatiens, white begonias) to highlight the pink tones. The large leaf size and warm pink coloration make this variety outstanding for massing under trees and in shaded foundation plantings where continuous warm-season color is desired from May through October.

common_name: Autumn Sun Toad Lily (Large White Spotted Late-Blooming Shade Perennial)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Autumn Sun has better sun tolerance than most toad lilies — it performs in positions with morning sun provided afternoon shade and consistent soil moisture are maintained.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Autumn Sun is a hybrid toad lily valued for its relatively upright habit, large white-and-purple spotted flowers, and better adaptability to sun and heat than pure Tricyrtis hirta selections. The intricate orchid-like flowers — white with distinctive purple spotting arranged in patterns across the petals and a prominent central column — bloom in late summer and fall (August–October in zone 9) at a time when most shade perennials have finished flowering, providing valuable late-season interest in shade positions that are bare of bloom by August. The flowers appear in the leaf axils along the arching to upright stems, creating a succession of bloom that extends over 4–6 weeks rather than a single flush. Tricyrtis 'Autumn Sun' produces broader, more strappy foliage than T. hirta and has slightly greater tolerance for warmer conditions — an advantage at the warm end of its range (zone 9) where summer heat can stress less adapted toad lily selections. In zone 9, toad lilies perform best in deep, consistently moist shade under canopy trees where summer temperatures are moderated; the fall blooming period occurs as temperatures cool toward the optimal range. Divide established clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor. An outstanding choice for naturalizing in moist, shaded garden positions where late-season bloom is a priority.
